title: On pseudogap phase as precursor to a superconducting dome in high-Tc cuprates: Non-analytic T* as a function of doping
abstract: We generalize the condition under which a quantum material exhibiting a pseudogap phase is a precursor to a superconducting (SC) dome. The result reveals the non-analytic T* as a function of doping. A well-known example is the high-Tc cuprates. Essentially, the SC dome is generated under two conditions: (1) that the pseudogap T* is a decreasing function of doping, due to the decrease in size of extended pairing of doped holes with doping, and most importantly, (2) that the rate of configurational-ordering parameter is an increasing function of doping as a result of the decrease in extended length of the disordered pairs. These two conditions are provided by the new entanglement and confinement pairing mechanism of high-Tc cuprates. This is a theory that has recently been discussed in the literature by Buot et al. It hinges on a novel strong entanglement and confinement hole pairing (ECHP) mechanism that unravels the microscopic features of the entire phase diagram of both electron and hole-doped high-Tc cuprates.
summary: This paper develops a generalized theory showing that the pseudogap phase can precede superconductivity if two doping-dependent conditions on pairing strength and ordering rates are met. It applies this framework to high-Tc cuprates using an entanglement and confinement pairing model, successfully explaining key features like the SC dome structure and strange metal behavior through changes in configurational entropy.
main_problem: To generalize the theoretical condition under which a quantum material exhibiting a pseudogap phase acts as a precursor to a superconducting (SC) dome.
main_result: The SC dome formation requires two conditions: decreasing $T^*$ with doping and an increasing configurational-ordering rate ($R$) with doping, explained by a novel entanglement and confinement pairing mechanism.
method: Theoretical derivation using macroscopic order parameters like Configuration Entropy (CE) to characterize the transition from local pair ordering ($	ext{PO}$) to global superconducting order ($	ext{CO}$).
model_or_system: The study focuses on high-Tc cuprates, modeling them with a novel strong entanglement and confinement hole pairing (ECHP) mechanism that governs the phase diagram across doping levels.
key_observables: $T^*$ (pseudogap temperature), $T_C$ (superconducting transition temperature), Configuration Entropy ($	ext{CE}$), and the configurational-ordering rate parameter ($R$).
important_parameters: Doping level ($d$), Temperature ($T$), Pseudogap/Superconducting gap magnitudes.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes a linear temperature dependence for the Configuration Entropy (CE) and relies on establishing two specific doping dependencies for $T^*$ and $R$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the generic phase diagram showing the SC dome, the relationship between $	ext{CE}$ and $T$ revealing singularities where $R 	o \infty$, and the coexistence of strange metal behavior above $T_C$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the general criteria for pseudogap precursors to superconductivity, applies these criteria specifically to high-Tc cuprates using the ECHP mechanism, derives the non-analytic relationship between $T^*$ and $T_C$, and discusses implications for strange metal behavior.
why_interesting: While focused on cuprates, the framework of analyzing phase transitions via entropy changes and rate parameters ($R$) provides a general theoretical tool applicable to understanding complex many-body dynamics in strongly correlated systems beyond standard BCS theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
